source: PSLE Science AL1 content pack, Harman Johll + Claude, April 2026
notes: Drop this into /lab/shared/content/al1_pack.json. Phase 3 ingest script merges this into the shared content module, enriching the existing glossary with templates and seeding Key Ideas and the question databank for Coach mode.

diversity.name: Diversity
diversity.tagline: How living and non-living things are classified by their characteristics.
keyIdeas: Living things share seven characteristics: they grow, reproduce, respond to stimuli, respire, need nutrients, excrete waste, and move.
Plants are classified into flowering plants (which reproduce by producing flowers and seeds) and non-flowering plants (which reproduce by spores or cones).
Animals are classified as vertebrates (which have a backbone) or invertebrates (which do not have a backbone).
Vertebrates are divided into five groups: mammals, birds, fish, reptiles, and amphibians. Each group has distinct characteristics across skin covering, young, breathing organs, and body temperature.
Mammals have fur or hair, give birth to live young, feed young with milk, breathe with lungs, and are warm-blooded.
Fungi and bacteria are living things but are neither plants nor animals, because they do not make their own food like plants, and they do not move or eat like animals.
Materials are classified by their properties: hardness, flexibility, strength, ability to float, waterproofness, and whether they allow light, heat, or electricity to pass through.
A single animal can share characteristics with more than one group, which can make classification difficult and requires looking at the full set of features.

terms.template: An animal that has ___.
terms.definition: An animal that has a backbone.

terms.template: An animal that does not have ___.
terms.definition: An animal that does not have a backbone.

terms.template: A vertebrate that has ___, gives birth to ___, feeds young with ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.
terms.definition: A vertebrate that has fur or hair, gives birth to live young, feeds young with milk, breathes with lungs, and is warm-blooded.

terms.template: A vertebrate that has ___, has ___, lays ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.
terms.definition: A vertebrate that has feathers, has a beak, lays eggs with hard shells, breathes with lungs, and is warm-blooded.

terms.template: A vertebrate that has ___, lives in ___, breathes through ___, and is ___.
terms.definition: A vertebrate that has scales, lives in water, breathes through gills, lays eggs, and is cold-blooded.

terms.template: A vertebrate that has ___, lays ___, breathes with ___, and is ___.
terms.definition: A vertebrate that has dry scaly skin, lays eggs with leathery shells, breathes with lungs, and is cold-blooded.

terms.template: A vertebrate that has ___, begins life in ___ with ___, develops ___ as an adult, and is ___.
terms.definition: A vertebrate that has moist smooth skin, begins life in water with gills, develops lungs as an adult, and is cold-blooded.

terms.template: An invertebrate that has ___ body parts and ___ legs.
terms.definition: An invertebrate that has three body parts (head, thorax, abdomen) and six legs.

terms.template: A plant that reproduces by producing ___ which develop into ___.
terms.definition: A plant that reproduces by producing flowers, which develop into fruits containing seeds.

terms.template: A plant that does not produce ___; it reproduces by ___.
terms.definition: A plant that does not produce flowers; it reproduces by spores or by cones.

questions.q: A scientist found an animal with fur, lungs, and that lays eggs. Suggest why it is difficult to classify this animal into one of the five vertebrate groups.
questions.model: The animal shares characteristics with more than one group. Its fur and lungs are characteristics of a mammal, but mammals give birth to live young, not lay eggs. The egg-laying is a characteristic of a bird or reptile, but these groups do not have fur. Because no single group matches all its features, it is difficult to classify.

questions.q: Explain why a fish cannot survive out of water for long.
questions.model: A fish breathes through its gills, which need to be kept wet in order to take in dissolved oxygen from the water. When a fish is out of water, the gills dry out and can no longer absorb oxygen, so the fish cannot respire and will die.

questions.q: Ravi looked at two living things under a microscope. He saw that one made its own food and the other did not. He concluded that the one making its own food is a plant. Suggest why his conclusion may not be correct.
questions.model: Making its own food is a characteristic of plants, but not all living things that make their own food are plants. For example, some bacteria also make their own food. To confirm that the living thing is a plant, Ravi would need to check for other plant characteristics, such as having chlorophyll in cells with cell walls.

questions.q: Suggest one way in which a reptile is similar to a fish, and one way in which they are different.
questions.model: Similarity: both reptiles and fish are cold-blooded, meaning their body temperature changes with the surroundings. Difference: reptiles breathe with lungs, while fish breathe through gills.

questions.q: Explain how the way flowering plants reproduce is different from the way non-flowering plants reproduce.
questions.model: Flowering plants reproduce by producing flowers, which undergo pollination and fertilisation to form seeds inside fruits. Non-flowering plants do not produce flowers and therefore do not produce seeds in this way; instead, they reproduce by spores (such as ferns) or by cones (such as pine trees).

cycles.name: Cycles
cycles.tagline: Life cycles, matter, and the water cycle: repeating patterns in nature.
keyIdeas: Every living thing goes through a life cycle: birth, growth, reproduction, and death. The cycle continues because offspring reproduce in turn.
Some animals undergo complete metamorphosis (butterfly, frog, mosquito, beetle). Their life cycle has four stages: egg, larva, pupa, adult. The young looks very different from the adult.
Some animals undergo incomplete metamorphosis (grasshopper, cockroach, dragonfly). Their life cycle has three stages: egg, nymph, adult. The nymph looks similar to the adult.
A flowering plant reproduces through four steps: pollination (pollen transferred from anther to stigma), fertilisation (pollen fuses with ovule), seed formation, and seed dispersal.
Seeds are dispersed by wind (light seeds with wings), water (seeds that float), animals (seeds with hooks or inside fruits), or explosive mechanism (pods that burst open).

Matter exists in three states: solid (fixed shape and volume), liquid (fixed volume but takes shape of container), gas (no fixed shape or volume). The particles are arranged and move differently in each state.
Matter changes state through heating (melting, evaporation, boiling) and cooling (freezing, condensation). These changes do not create or destroy matter; only its state changes.
Evaporation happens at any temperature below boiling, at the surface of a liquid. Boiling happens only at the boiling point and throughout the liquid. Both turn liquid into gas.

The water cycle is the continuous movement of water between Earth and the atmosphere: evaporation from oceans, lakes, and rivers; condensation into clouds; precipitation as rain; collection in water bodies.

terms.template: The series of stages a living thing goes through from ___ to ___ to ___.
terms.definition: The series of stages a living thing goes through from birth, through growth and reproduction, to death.

terms.template: A life cycle with ___ stages, where the young looks ___ from the adult.
terms.definition: A life cycle with four stages (egg, larva, pupa, adult), where the young looks very different from the adult.

terms.template: A life cycle with ___ stages, where the young looks ___ to the adult.
terms.definition: A life cycle with three stages (egg, nymph, adult), where the young looks similar to the adult.

terms.template: The transfer of ___ from the ___ to the ___ of a flower.
terms.definition: The transfer of pollen grains from the anther (male part) to the stigma (female part) of a flower.

terms.template: The fusion of ___ with ___ to form ___.
terms.definition: The fusion of the male reproductive cell (pollen nucleus) with the female reproductive cell (ovule) to form a seed.

terms.template: The process by which ___ develops into ___.
terms.definition: The process by which a seed develops into a new young plant.

terms.template: The process by which ___ changes to ___ at ___.
terms.definition: The process by which a liquid changes to a gas at any temperature below its boiling point, occurring at the surface of the liquid.

terms.template: The process by which ___ changes to ___ when ___.
terms.definition: The process by which a gas changes to a liquid when it loses heat and cools down.

terms.template: The process by which ___ changes to ___ when ___.
terms.definition: The process by which a solid changes to a liquid when it gains heat.

terms.template: The rapid change of ___ to ___ throughout the liquid, at a ___.
terms.definition: The rapid change of a liquid to a gas throughout the liquid, at a specific temperature called the boiling point.

terms.template: The continuous movement of water between ___ and ___ through ___.
terms.definition: The continuous movement of water between Earth and the atmosphere through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.

questions.q: A farmer wants her tomato plants to reproduce and produce new seeds. Describe the sequence of events that must happen for new tomato seeds to form.
questions.model: First, pollination occurs: pollen from the anther is transferred to the stigma of the flower, usually by insects or wind. Next, fertilisation occurs: the pollen nucleus travels down to the ovary and fuses with the ovule. After fertilisation, the ovule develops into a seed, and the ovary develops into a fruit containing the seed.

questions.q: When Alexey comes out of a swimming pool on a hot day, he feels cold even though the air is warm. Explain why.
questions.model: The water on his skin evaporates, changing from liquid to gas. Evaporation requires heat, and this heat is taken from his body. As his skin loses heat, he feels cold.

questions.q: Why do some plants have fruits with hooks on the outside?
questions.model: The hooks allow the fruits to attach to the fur of passing animals. The animals then carry the fruits away from the parent plant and drop them in a new location, where the seeds can germinate. This is dispersal by animals, and it spreads the seeds so that young plants do not compete with the parent plant for sunlight, water, and nutrients.

questions.q: On a humid morning, water droplets appear on the outside of a cold drink can. Explain how the water droplets formed.
questions.model: Water vapour is present in the surrounding air. When the water vapour touches the cold surface of the can, it loses heat and cools down. The water vapour then condenses, changing from a gas to a liquid, and forms water droplets on the outside of the can.

questions.q: Describe one similarity and two differences between evaporation and boiling.
questions.model: Similarity: both evaporation and boiling are processes in which a liquid changes to a gas. Differences: evaporation takes place only at the surface of the liquid while boiling takes place throughout the liquid. Evaporation can happen at any temperature below the boiling point, while boiling only happens at the boiling point.

systems.name: Systems
systems.tagline: How parts work together: in plants, in humans, and in electrical circuits.
keyIdeas: A system is made up of parts that work together to perform a function. If a part is missing or broken, the system cannot work properly.
The plant transport system carries water and mineral salts from the roots through the stem (via the xylem) to the leaves. It also carries food made in the leaves to the rest of the plant.

Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide, producing food (in the form of starch) and releasing oxygen. It takes place in the leaves, in cells containing chlorophyll.
The human digestive system breaks down food into smaller, soluble substances that can be absorbed into the blood. The path is: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and anus.

The human respiratory system takes in oxygen and releases carbon dioxide. Air travels through the nose, trachea, bronchi, and into the lungs, where oxygen passes into the blood and carbon dioxide leaves the blood.
The human circulatory system is a network of the heart, blood, and blood vessels. The heart pumps blood around the body, carrying oxygen and digested food to all cells and carrying waste away.

Human systems work together: the digestive system absorbs nutrients, the respiratory system supplies oxygen, the circulatory system transports both to every cell so that the cells can respire and release energy.

An electrical circuit is a complete path through which electric current can flow. A circuit must contain a source of electrical energy (battery or cell) and a conducting path, usually with a component such as a bulb.
A material is a conductor if it allows electricity (or heat) to pass through easily, and an insulator if it does not. Metals are good conductors; plastic, rubber, and wood are insulators.

terms.template: A group of parts that ___ together to ___.
terms.definition: A group of parts that work together to perform a specific function.

terms.template: The process by which green plants make ___ using ___, ___, and ___, producing ___.
terms.definition: The process by which green plants make food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide, producing food in the form of starch and releasing oxygen.

terms.template: The green substance in ___ that absorbs ___ for ___.
terms.definition: The green substance in plant cells (found in leaves) that absorbs light energy from the Sun for photosynthesis.

terms.template: The process of breaking down ___ into ___ that can be ___.
terms.definition: The process of breaking down food into smaller, soluble substances that can be absorbed into the blood.

terms.template: The process by which living things release ___ from ___ by using ___.
terms.definition: The process by which living things release energy from food by using oxygen.

terms.template: The movement of ___ around the body, carrying ___ to cells and ___ from cells.
terms.definition: The movement of blood around the body, carrying oxygen and digested food to cells and carrying waste materials away from cells.

terms.template: A complete path through which ___ can flow.
terms.definition: A complete path through which electric current can flow.

terms.template: A material that allows ___ to pass through ___.
terms.definition: A material that allows electricity or heat to pass through it easily.

terms.template: A material that does not allow ___ to pass through ___.
terms.definition: A material that does not allow electricity or heat to pass through it easily.

questions.q: Explain why a plant needs both water and sunlight to survive.
questions.model: A plant needs water and sunlight for photosynthesis, the process by which it makes food. Sunlight provides the energy, which is absorbed by chlorophyll. Water, together with carbon dioxide, is a raw material that is converted into food (starch). Without either water or sunlight, the plant cannot make food and will eventually die.

questions.q: A green plant was placed in a dark cupboard for two weeks. Describe and explain what would happen to the plant.
questions.model: The plant would turn yellow and eventually die. In the dark, the plant cannot receive sunlight, so photosynthesis cannot take place. The plant is unable to make food. Once the food stored in the plant is used up, the plant has no energy to carry out life processes and dies.

questions.q: Alexey noticed that his heart beats faster after he runs. Explain why.
questions.model: When Alexey runs, his muscles work harder and need more energy. To release more energy, the muscle cells need more oxygen and digested food. The heart beats faster to pump more blood around the body, so that oxygen and digested food are delivered to the muscle cells more quickly, and waste carbon dioxide is removed more quickly.

questions.q: Explain how the digestive system and the circulatory system work together to keep the body alive.
questions.model: The digestive system breaks down food into small, soluble substances in the small intestine. These digested substances are absorbed into the blood. The circulatory system then transports the digested food in the blood to all the cells of the body. The cells use the digested food, together with oxygen, to release energy for life processes.

questions.q: In a circuit with a cell, a wire, a bulb, and a switch, the bulb does not light up when the switch is closed. Suggest two possible reasons.
questions.model: First, the circuit may not be complete: a wire could be loose or broken, so current cannot flow through the bulb. Second, the cell may be flat, meaning it no longer provides electrical energy to the circuit. A third possibility is that the bulb is blown, so current cannot pass through its filament.

interactions.name: Interactions
interactions.tagline: How forces act on objects, and how living things affect each other.
keyIdeas: A force is a push or a pull. A force can change the shape, speed, or direction of an object, and can start or stop motion.
Gravitational force is the force that pulls objects towards the centre of the Earth. Weight is the gravitational force acting on an object.

Frictional force is a force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact. It acts in the opposite direction to motion and depends on the roughness of the surfaces and the weight pressing them together.
Elastic spring force is the force produced by a stretched or compressed elastic object, acting to return the object to its original shape.
Magnetic force is the force exerted between a magnet and a magnetic material, or between two magnets. It can act at a distance.
In any situation, several forces may act on an object at the same time. The overall effect depends on the size and direction of each force.
A food chain shows how energy is passed from one living thing to another, starting with a producer (usually a green plant) and moving to consumers.
A food web is made up of many interconnected food chains in the same habitat. A change to one organism can affect many others.
An adaptation is a feature that helps an organism to survive in its habitat. Adaptations can be structural (body features) or behavioural (actions).
Human activities such as deforestation, pollution, and over-hunting disrupt food chains and damage habitats. Conservation efforts protect species and ecosystems.

terms.template: A ___ or ___ that can change the ___, ___, or ___ of an object.
terms.definition: A push or a pull that can change the shape, speed, or direction of an object.

terms.template: The force that pulls objects towards ___.
terms.definition: The force that pulls objects towards the centre of the Earth.

terms.template: A force that opposes ___ between ___.
terms.definition: A force that opposes motion between two surfaces that are in contact.

terms.template: The force produced by ___ that tries to return to ___.
terms.definition: The force produced by a stretched or compressed elastic object that tries to return to its original shape.

terms.template: The force between ___ and ___, or between ___.
terms.definition: The force between a magnet and a magnetic material, or between two magnets.

terms.template: An organism that makes its own food by ___.
terms.definition: An organism, usually a green plant, that makes its own food through photosynthesis.

terms.template: An organism that gets food by ___.
terms.definition: An organism that gets food by eating other organisms.

terms.template: A sequence that shows how ___ is passed from ___ to ___.
terms.definition: A sequence that shows how energy (in the form of food) is passed from one organism to another.

terms.template: A ___ of an organism that helps it to ___.
terms.definition: A feature or behaviour of an organism that helps it to survive in its habitat.

questions.q: A ball is kicked up into the air. It eventually slows down, stops, and falls back to the ground. Explain using the forces acting on the ball.
questions.model: When the ball is kicked, it moves upward. Gravitational force acts on the ball, pulling it downward towards the Earth. This force slows the ball down as it moves up, until it stops momentarily at its highest point. Gravitational force continues to act, pulling the ball back down to the ground.

questions.q: Why is it easier to push a heavy box on a smooth floor than on a rough carpet?
questions.model: Frictional force opposes the motion of the box. The rough carpet produces a larger frictional force between the box and the surface than the smooth floor does. Since more force is needed to overcome the larger frictional force of the carpet, it is harder to push the box on the carpet and easier to push it on the smooth floor.

questions.q: Alexey noticed that the brake pads on his bicycle wear out more quickly when he rides on rough roads than on smooth roads. Explain why.
questions.model: When Alexey applies the brakes, frictional force acts between the brake pads and the wheel to slow the bicycle down. On rough roads, the bicycle shakes more and the brakes are used more often or harder, so there is more friction over time. The greater friction wears away the brake pads more quickly.

questions.q: Explain how a polar bear is adapted to survive in the cold Arctic environment.
questions.model: A polar bear has thick fur and a layer of fat under its skin. The thick fur traps a layer of air, which is a poor conductor of heat, reducing heat loss from the body. The layer of fat also acts as an insulator, keeping the bear warm in the cold Arctic. Its white fur also helps it blend in with the snow to catch prey.

questions.q: A farmer sprays insecticide on his crops to kill caterpillars. The insecticide also kills many other insects. Explain how this could affect the birds in the area.
questions.model: Many of the insects in the area are food for the birds. When the insecticide kills the insects, the birds have less food available. Some birds may not have enough to eat, and their population may decrease. Birds that feed their young on insects may not be able to raise them, further reducing the population.

energy.name: Energy
energy.tagline: Forms of energy, heat and light, and how energy is converted.
keyIdeas: Energy is the ability to do work. It exists in many forms: light, heat, sound, electrical, kinetic (motion), and potential (stored).
Potential energy can be gravitational (due to an object's height above the ground) or elastic (stored in a stretched or compressed object).
Energy can be converted from one form to another, but it cannot be created or destroyed.
The Sun is the main source of energy on Earth. Energy from the Sun drives photosynthesis, the water cycle, and weather.
Renewable energy sources (Sun, wind, moving water) can be replaced naturally. Non-renewable sources (coal, oil, gas) cannot be replaced within a human lifetime.
Heat is a form of energy. Heat always flows from a hotter object to a colder object, until both reach the same temperature.
Heat can be transferred in three ways: by conduction (through solids), by convection (through liquids and gases), and by radiation (through empty space).
Heat has observable effects: it causes materials to expand when heated and contract when cooled, and it can change the state of matter.
Light travels in straight lines. When light hits a surface, it can be reflected, absorbed, or transmitted, depending on the material.
A shadow forms when an opaque object blocks light. The shape of a shadow depends on the shape of the object, and the size depends on how close the object is to the light source.

terms.template: The ability to ___.
terms.definition: The ability to do work.

terms.template: The energy of ___.
terms.definition: The energy possessed by a moving object; the energy of motion.

terms.template: Energy stored in an object due to its ___ or ___.
terms.definition: Energy stored in an object due to its position or its condition.

terms.template: Energy stored in an object due to its ___ above ___.
terms.definition: Energy stored in an object due to its height above the ground.

terms.template: Energy stored in an object that is ___ or ___.
terms.definition: Energy stored in an object that is stretched or compressed.

terms.template: A form of energy that flows from ___ to ___.
terms.definition: A form of energy that flows from a hotter object to a colder object.

terms.template: A measure of how ___ or ___ something is.
terms.definition: A measure of how hot or cold something is.

terms.template: A material that allows heat to pass through ___.
terms.definition: A material that allows heat to pass through it easily.

terms.template: A material that does not allow heat to pass through ___.
terms.definition: A material that does not allow heat to pass through it easily.

terms.template: A material that does not allow ___ to pass through.
terms.definition: A material that does not allow light to pass through.

terms.template: A material that allows ___ light to pass through, but objects cannot be ___.
terms.definition: A material that allows some light to pass through, but objects cannot be seen clearly through it.

terms.template: A material that allows ___ light to pass through, and objects can be ___.
terms.definition: A material that allows nearly all light to pass through, and objects can be seen clearly through it.

questions.q: A roller coaster car is pulled to the top of the first hill. It is then released and rolls down. Describe the energy changes that take place from the top of the hill to the bottom.
questions.model: At the top of the hill, the car has maximum gravitational potential energy and minimum kinetic energy. As the car rolls down, its height decreases, so gravitational potential energy decreases. At the same time, the car moves faster, so kinetic energy increases. At the bottom of the hill, the car has minimum gravitational potential energy and maximum kinetic energy.

questions.q: Alexey touched a metal spoon and a wooden spoon, both at the same room temperature. He said the metal spoon felt colder. Explain why.
questions.model: The metal spoon and the wooden spoon are both at room temperature, which is lower than Alexey's body temperature. Metal is a good conductor of heat, so it conducts heat away from Alexey's hand quickly, making the spoon feel cold. Wood is a poor conductor of heat, so it does not conduct heat away as quickly, and therefore feels less cold.

questions.q: When a pot of water is heated on a stove, bubbles form at the bottom of the pot and rise to the surface. Explain how the bubbles form and why they rise.
questions.model: When the water at the bottom of the pot is heated, it gains enough heat to reach its boiling point. The water changes from a liquid to a gas (water vapour), forming bubbles. The bubbles rise to the surface because water vapour is less dense than liquid water.

questions.q: Alexey noticed that his shadow is longer in the late afternoon than at noon. Explain why.
questions.model: The size of a shadow depends on the angle of the light source. At noon, the Sun is high in the sky, almost overhead, so the light hits Alexey from a steep angle and his shadow is short. In the late afternoon, the Sun is lower in the sky, so the light hits him from a low angle, casting a longer shadow on the ground.

questions.q: Why are pots and pans often made with metal bodies and plastic handles?
questions.model: The body of the pot needs to transfer heat quickly from the stove to the food. Metal is a good conductor of heat, so it allows the food to be cooked quickly. The handle needs to stay cool enough to hold safely. Plastic is a poor conductor of heat (an insulator), so heat does not pass easily from the pot to the hand, and the handle stays safe to hold.

megaMapSeed.notes: Starter data for the mega concept map. Phase 2 expands this with 80+ phenomena across all topics and types all cross-theme edges.

exampleCrossThemeLinks.reason: Plants convert sunlight energy into chemical energy stored in food
Plants are producers at the base of every food chain
Evaporation is a step in the water cycle
Evaporation requires heat energy from the surroundings
Friction converts kinetic energy to heat energy
Metals tend to be good conductors of both electricity and heat
Oxygen for respiration is carried by the circulatory system
